headline: Wyoming lawmakers exempt two UW programs, one facility from proposed $40 million cut
summary: The Wyoming Joint Appropriations Committee finalized a draft budget that includes a $40 million reduction (11%) to the University of Wyoming's block grant, bringing total cuts and funding denials to nearly $61 million; the committee exempted specific programs including the WORTH Institute and High Bay Research Facility from these cuts, which critics argue will disproportionately burden other university areas while supporters claim it aligns with the land-grant mission; these budget decisions reflect increasing legislative control over university autonomy; the full Legislature will consider the proposed cuts when it convenes Monday.

headline: Cheyenne Roundup 2026: Episode 1 | How Wyoming’s budget gets made
summary: Despite a revenue surplus, Wyoming's 68th Legislature faces a contentious budget session as the Joint Appropriations Committee proposes eliminating the Wyoming Business Council and cutting the University of Wyoming's block grant by $40 million, threatening 160 jobs. The Freedom Caucus, controlling the House but not the Senate, aims to reduce spending to pre-COVID levels while Gov. Mark Gordon recommended adding $250 million to savings. With only line-item veto power, the governor cannot restore funding. If the chambers fail to agree, a special session would be required. The 2024 session required a rare second joint conference committee after the first failed to reach agreement.

headline: Rock Springs senator seeks lengthy prison sentences for impeding federal agents
summary: Sen. John Kolb (R-Rock Springs) is drafting legislation to make impeding federal immigration enforcement agents a felony punishable by 5-25 years in prison, a penalty more severe than aggravated vehicular homicide. The bill was prompted by a January incident where protesters blocked ICE agents at a Rock Springs hotel. Because the upcoming legislative session is budget-focused, the bill would require a two-thirds majority vote for introduction. Sen. Tara Nethercott is separately exploring enhancements to existing interference with peace officer laws as an alternative approach. Kolb emphasized his strong support for law enforcement and distinguished Wyoming's stance from other states like Minnesota.

headline: Legislative notebook: Idaho lawmakers vote to cut state budget, conform to ‘big, beautiful’ law
summary: Idaho's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee voted 14-6 to cut the state budget by 1% ($131M) for FY2026 and 13-7 to cut 2% ($143M) for FY2027, resulting in 5% total reductions for most agencies after Gov. Little's prior 3% holdback. These cuts offset revenue losses from conforming to federal tax changes under Trump's One Big Beautiful Bill Act, costing Idaho $155M in FY2026 and $175M in FY2027. K-12 education, Idaho State Police, and the Department of Correction are exempt. Agency reports warn of furloughs, hiring freezes, reduced programs, and tuition hikes, with the University of Idaho stating further cuts would permanently reduce capacity in critical fields like cybersecurity and nuclear engineering. JFAC will vote on maintenance budgets Feb. 13.

headline: Idaho Legislature’s budget committee approves additional budget cuts for most state agencies
summary: The Idaho Legislature's Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee voted 14-6 to approve additional 1% cuts ($131M) for FY2026 and 13-7 for permanent 2% cuts ($143M) for FY2027, resulting in total cuts of approximately 4% and 5% for most state agencies. These reductions build upon Governor Little's existing 3% cuts and exclude K-12 schools, Medicaid, corrections, and state police. Republican co-chairs stated the cuts are necessary for structural budget balance, maintaining a year-end surplus, and accommodating federal tax policy changes. Legislative opponents criticized the across-the-board approach as a "chainsaw" tactic that is short-sighted and undermines quality of life. State agencies warned the cuts could force employee furloughs, jeopardize medical education programs, impair constitutional public defender services, and reduce park operations.

headline: ICE detention of DACA recipient ripples through mixed-status Nebraska family
summary: DACA recipient Joel Angel-Becerril (27) has been detained by ICE and denied a bond hearing during deportation proceedings. ACLU Nebraska filed a federal lawsuit challenging a new ICE policy interpretation that makes nearly all detained immigrants ineligible for bond. The case reflects the Trump administration's ramped-up deportation strategy and represents Nebraska's first known detention of a DACA recipient. Angel-Becerril, who has no criminal convictions, was arrested immediately after an assault charge was dismissed. The detention impacts his mixed-status family, leaving his 22-year-old brother as breadwinner for their U.S. citizen siblings.

headline: After loss of tax credits, WA sees a drop in insurance coverage
summary: Washington state's Cascade Care Savings program is providing premium assistance to 118,000 residents to mitigate the loss of federal health insurance subsidies, but officials declare this assistance unsustainable beyond 2026, creating significant budget jeopardy for 2027-2029 as the state faces pressure to maintain coverage for low-income enrollees while federal policy changes continue to strain resources.

headline: DHS again denies Minnesota members of Congress access to Whipple building
summary: U.S. Reps. Angie Craig and Betty McCollum were denied entry to the Whipple Federal Building detention facility for immigrants and citizens, while Rep. Kelly Morrison was permitted access; officials cited a 13-visitor cap that the lawmakers claim violates federal law granting congressional oversight authority. Craig raised concerns that extended waiting periods may allow staff to improperly prepare facilities before inspections. The denial follows a federal judge's ruling that Homeland Security likely broke the law by blocking congressional visits without notice, resulting in a temporary restraining order. The incident highlights ongoing tensions between congressional oversight responsibilities and immigration enforcement operations. McCollum and Craig vowed to continue pursuing their statutory oversight duties.

headline: Vermont hospitals say collaboration is saving them money. Now, they’re joining forces.
summary: Vermont hospitals are advancing Act 167's transformation through cross-network collaboration: Gifford Health Care coordinates patient routing with Dartmouth and UVM Health networks to double inpatient capacity; UVM Health is developing "fractional specialist" sharing programs for smaller hospitals; and the state is deploying $195 million in federal Rural Health Transformation Funds for mobile dental units, expanded EMT home treatment, and mental health urgent cares.

headline: ‘They’ll never take our public lands from our public hands’: U.S. Sen. Heinrich rallies Roundhouse
summary: "Camo at the Capitol Day" at the New Mexico Roundhouse brought together conservation advocates and lawmakers including U.S. Sen. Martin Heinrich to support public lands protection and the Land of Enchantment Legacy Fund, representing a significant public gathering for political advocacy on environmental policy.

headline: Rising anti-ICE sentiment seen in NJ special primary results
summary: In New Jersey's 11th Congressional District Democratic primary, progressive organizer Analilia Mejia leads former Rep. Tom Malinowski after an outside group spent millions attacking Malinowski's 2019 vote to fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE); Mejia campaigned on abolishing ICE, calling it a dangerous institution that cannot be reformed, while ICE raids in Morristown and a proposed detention center in Roxbury galvanized district Democrats against any candidate perceived as supporting the agency, demonstrating how immigration enforcement policy has become a decisive electoral mobilization issue.

headline: Supreme Court nominee lays out philosophy, says he won’t recuse himself from school funding case
summary: Judge Daniel Will, nominated to the New Hampshire Supreme Court, indicated he would likely not recuse himself from the Rand school funding lawsuit despite previously defending the state in the similar ConVal case as solicitor general, arguing recusals apply to cases, not issues. The Supreme Court's 2025 ConVal decision ruled in favor of school districts claiming state funding was unconstitutionally low, but occurred under unusual circumstances with two justices recused. Will's participation in Rand, alongside Chief Justice MacDonald who also declined recusal, could shift future K-12 education funding rulings. The case involves property-tax payers challenging state adequacy payments to schools. Will's stance underscores how judicial recusal discretion significantly impacts education funding policy.

headline: American Prairie, Tribal coalition files protest over rescinded grazing rights
summary: American Prairie and the Coalition of Large Tribes filed protests against the BLM's proposed cancellation of bison grazing permits in Phillips County, Montana, a decision American Prairie decried as "unlawful" and driven by political pressure from state officials; the move would reverse a 40-year BLM precedent, disrupting American Prairie's 900-animal herd and threatening tribal buffalo programs for Montana's Fort Belknap Indian Community and Chippewa Cree Tribe, as well as future leases for California tribes.

headline: Family of still-missing camper sues to shut down Camp Mystic
summary: The July 4, 2025 flood memorial cross at Camp Mystic could catalyze protests if victims' families and advocates rally there to demand government disaster accountability, flood policy reforms, and infrastructure improvements along the Guadalupe River.

headline: U.S. Reps. Maxwell Frost & Darren Soto tell Kristi Noem not to open ICE facility in Central Florida
summary: Two Democratic U.S. Representatives from Central Florida oppose a planned $100 million ICE detention center in Orlando, warning it would tear families apart and propagate suffering. Republican U.S. Representative and gubernatorial candidate Byron Donalds supports the facility as essential for deporting "millions of criminal illegals." Orlando's city attorney confirmed local officials cannot block the center due to the Federal Supremacy Clause, rendering them legally powerless despite opposition from Democratic gubernatorial candidates Jerry Demings and David Jolly.

headline: Local, state officials vet plans to secure Greenwood Leflore Hospital’s financial future
summary: Greenwood Leflore Hospital, serving 300,000 patients in the medically underserved Mississippi Delta, faces imminent closure over a $3.5 million Medicaid debt from overpayments, prompting state lawmakers to advance legislation extending repayment timelines and appropriating $10 million to facilitate a potential sale. The Mississippi Division of Medicaid maintains the recoupment is routine reconciliation, while hospital officials warn the repayment schedule would force closure and eliminate critical healthcare access. Political debate intensifies as some legislators advocate for Medicaid expansion as the only sustainable solution for the hospital's large uninsured patient population, and federal Medicaid cuts beginning in 2028 under recent legislation threaten further funding reductions.

headline: Byhalia ‘ICE warehouse’ deal is canned after chat with Noem, Wicker tweets
summary: DHS Secretary Kristi Noem has abandoned plans to convert a Byhalia, Mississippi warehouse into an ICE detention center capable of holding 8,500 immigrants, following opposition from U.S. Senator Roger Wicker and local officials who cited infrastructure concerns and the loss of a key industrial development site; the proposal was part of a broader $45 billion ICE detention expansion plan under the Trump administration, which has allocated a record $85 billion to the agency, sparking community protests and planned formal objections before the county board.

headline: With no-masks proposal, Jackson County joins push to restrain ICE
summary: Jackson County, Missouri is considering an ordinance requiring federal immigration agents to keep faces/badges visible, mirroring national efforts to ban masks for law enforcement. Supporters argue it will increase accountability and deescalate tensions, while Homeland Security cites the Supremacy Clause to oppose such bans. Legal experts are divided on constitutionality, and Jackson County Sheriff Darryl Forté warns the ordinance could be unenforceable and create dangerous confrontations. The issue has become politically charged, with Democrats pushing for mask bans in federal budget negotiations while Missouri Senator Eric Schmitt strongly opposes restrictions on immigration enforcement. California already passed a similar law being challenged by the federal government, highlighting the broader state-federal conflict.

headline: More people in SC are getting vaccinated for measles, but cases continue to spread
summary: South Carolina's measles outbreak—the nation's largest at 920 cases—has prompted a 72% increase in statewide MMR vaccinations and a 162% spike in Spartanburg County, with nearly 1,200 infants receiving early doses. Democratic Sen. Margie Bright Matthews proposed legislation eliminating religious exemptions for school vaccine requirements, mandating both MMR doses for children ages 1-5. Attorney General Alan Wilson condemned the bill as governmental overreach violating parental rights and bodily autonomy, making passage unlikely in a state that banned public employer vaccine mandates. Religious exemptions have surged post-COVID from under 1% to 3.3% of students, with less than 90% of K-12 students submitting completed vaccination forms.

headline: Judge gives DMV five days to produce communications with ICE, Homeland Security
summary: Carson City District Judge Kristin Luis is considering civil penalties against the Nevada DMV for failing to comply with ACLU public records requests regarding communications with ICE and DHS, including encrypted Signal chats. The DMV initially denied working with immigration authorities but later admitted sending documents to them for verification. The judge ordered the agency to produce unredacted names and emails of individuals corresponding with federal immigration officials, provide missing group emails, and submit a sworn declaration of its policies for interacting with immigration authorities. ACLU attorneys argued the DMV's redactions and delays violate Nevada's Public Records Act, while the agency claimed privacy concerns. The case highlights tensions between government transparency, state-federal immigration enforcement coordination, and privacy rights.

headline: RI lawmakers fast-track $18M reserve to save Roger Williams, Fatima hospitals
summary: Rhode Island lawmakers are fast-tracking an $18 million state backstop to prevent closure of Roger Williams Medical Center and Our Lady of Fatima Hospital, which serve 300,000 patients annually across 500 beds and 55,000 emergency visits. State Health Director Dr. Jerry Larkin warned the shutdown would be "catastrophic" for the state's fragile healthcare system, prompting legislative leaders and the governor to expedite approval before a Feb. 27 bankruptcy deadline while regulators imposed 85 conditions requiring extensive financial reporting.

headline: Pritzker’s new budget report could set tone for conservative 2027 spending plan
summary: The provided article contains no information regarding Illinois' state budget, deficits, surpluses, revenue forecasts, or potential budget modifications.

headline: Maine’s first measles case since 2019 reported in Penobscot County
summary: Maine CDC confirmed the state's first measles case since 2019 in an adult who visited St. Joseph's Hospital in Bangor while infectious Jan. 28-Feb. 5, marking New England's first 2026 case amid 733 nationwide infections across 20 states. The highly contagious disease, declared eliminated in 2000, has infected over 2,671 people since January 2025, with 90% of non-immune contacts at risk. Maine CDC is urging vaccination, emphasizing the MMR vaccine's 97% effectiveness, as public health officials monitor healthcare facility exposures.

headline: Gubernatorial candidates emphasize K-12 funding, disagree on response to ICE in schools at MEA forum
summary: At a Michigan Education Association forum, gubernatorial candidates presented divergent positions on ICE enforcement. Republican Mike Cox dismissed ICE as a classroom issue and called Trump's immigration policies a "straw man." Independent Mike Duggan claimed Detroit protected residents from ICE while denying sanctuary city status, drawing Democratic criticism. Democrat Chris Swanson advocated limiting ICE actions at schools and houses of worship. Democrat Jocelyn Benson pledged moral courage to protect communities from federal immigration tactics.

headline: Crow, Neguse visit Aurora ICE facility after court ruling restored right to unannounced visits
summary: Colorado Democratic Reps. Jason Crow and Joe Neguse conducted an unannounced oversight visit to the Aurora ICE detention facility after a federal judge temporarily blocked a Trump administration policy restricting such congressional visits; Neguse is the lead plaintiff in that lawsuit. Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had required seven days' notice, claiming facilities funded by the One Big Beautiful Bill Act are exempt from the 2019 law allowing unannounced visits. The lawmakers cited 32 ICE custody deaths in 2025 and last year being the deadliest for federal detention facilities in decades to demand transparency and accountability. The facility, privately operated by GEO Group, currently holds 1,376 detainees of its 1,530 capacity. The visit occurs amid growing national backlash to the administration's mass deportation efforts and concerns about lawlessness in immigration enforcement.

headline: ICE detention cases begin weaving through North Dakota courts
summary: ICE detentions in North Dakota have surged, with federal court filings rising from zero to seven emergency petitions in the past three months and arrests increasing 150% (202 vs. 81) year-over-year. The Trump administration's July rollback of 1997 bond hearing guidance has created a judicial split: U.S. District Judge Daniel Hovland ruled the practice violates federal law and ordered bond hearings, while Judge Dan Traynor upheld the executive branch's interpretation and denied multiple petitions. Only one North Dakota immigration attorney, Sue Swanson, practices in federal court to challenge detentions, highlighting a critical shortage of legal representation in a system that does not guarantee counsel. Nonprofits report widespread fear among immigrant communities, including lawful residents, prompting advice to avoid social media and maintain low profiles. The policy shift has left even those released from detention facing uncertain immigration court proceedings with limited legal review opportunities.

headline: After Minneapolis immigration crackdown, Birkebeiner director says foreign skiers express concerns
summary: Foreign participants in Wisconsin's American Birkebeiner ski race are expressing safety concerns after a federal immigration crackdown in Minnesota and the fatal shootings of two Minneapolis residents by immigration agents; international skiers are questioning whether it is safe to fly into Minneapolis and requesting contact numbers in case they are stopped by immigration officers, reflecting broader anxiety about President Trump's "American First" policies—including a crackdown on immigrants and suspension of visa programs—and highlighting potential impacts on international tourism and event attendance.

headline: Morrisey appoints Baylous to lead WV Homeland Security
summary: The appointment of veteran US Marshal Michael Baylous as West Virginia's Department of Homeland Security secretary may enhance state-federal coordination on immigration enforcement due to his extensive federal and state law enforcement background.

headline: House panel advances bills limiting ICE activity in Virginia
summary: A Virginia Democratic House subcommittee advanced a package of bills restricting ICE operations, requiring judicial warrants for civil immigration arrests in courthouses and limiting enforcement near schools, hospitals, and polling places. The measures would bar state and local law enforcement from assisting federal immigration authorities without judicial warrants and impose penalties on officers who conceal identities or impersonate federal agents. Multiple bills were consolidated, including protections for court attendees, witnesses, and families traveling to court proceedings. The legislative action follows Governor Spanberger's Executive Order ending state police cooperation with ICE and responds to reports of masked agents conducting warrantless courthouse arrests since January 2025. The bills aim to restore public trust and ensure immigrant communities can access essential civic services without fear of deportation.
